data.title: Rio Tinto expects Resolution Copper mine to open by mid-2030s
data.description:
HOUSTON, March 24 (Reuters) - Rio Tinto expects to open Arizona's Resolution Copper mine by the mid-2030s, a senior executive of the miner told Reuters on Tuesday.

The Anglo-Australian mining giant this month gained control of acreage needed to build one of the world's largest copper mines after a years-long court fight.

The company has now begun a $500 million drilling campaign to explore the 30% of the deposit it previously could not access. That research will help the company decide its production timeline, but first copper is expected within a decade.

"We are quite committed to bringing copper on as quickly as we can," Katie Jackson, head of Rio's copper business, told Reuters on the sidelines of the CERAWeek by S&P Global conference in Houston. "This is something we want to do in the early to mid 2030s."

Rio operates Utah's Kennecott copper mine and smelter, with all of its production consumed inside the country. The only other U.S. copper smelter is operated by Freeport-McMoRan.

(Reporting by Ernest Scheyder; Editing by David Gregorio)

By Ernest Scheyder

OpenAI's nonprofit arm names leaders, plans to spend at least $1 billion over next year
data.description:
March 24 (Reuters) - OpenAI on Tuesday announced key leadership appointments for its nonprofit arm and committed to investing at least $1 billion through the division over the next year in AI-related projects following a major restructuring at the ChatGPT-maker.

The OpenAI Foundation's spending will focus on measures including life sciences and medical research and workforce and community programs, OpenAI said.

In October, it announced a restructuring that allowed the company to move away from its nonprofit roots, likely to go public and help unlock more financing. The deal transformed OpenAI into a public benefit corporation controlled by a nonprofit with a stake in its financial success.

The company said on Tuesday that Robert Kaiden was joining the foundation as CFO. Kaiden has previously held senior leadership positions at Deloitte, Twitter - now called X - and luxury travel company Inspirato.

Through the investment, OpenAI will partner with institutions to use AI to research and develop treatments for Alzheimer's. It will also focus on high-mortality disease areas that do not receive enough funding, the company said.

It also said it would fund the development of AI safety measures for children and youth as well as community initiatives.

OpenAI co-founder Wojciech Zaremba is joining the foundation to lead the company's AI safety work, while Jacob Trefethen - from philanthropic funding and advisory firm Coefficient Giving - will head the foundation's life sciences efforts.

(Reporting by Deborah Sophia in Bengaluru; Editing by Maju Samuel and Pooja Desai)

data.title: Volkswagen to shift production to missile defense at one factory in deal with Israeli company, FT reports
US Treasury two-year yield extends rise after soft auction, now up 12.4 bps at 3.953%
Oil majors boost FTSE 100 amid war uncertainty
data.description:
(Alliance News) - The FTSE 100 closed higher on Tuesday, lifted by gains in oil majors BP and Shell, as investors await clarity over developments in the Middle East.

The FTSE 100 index closed up 71.01 points, 0.7%, at 9,965.16.

The FTSE 250 ended down 110.89 points, 0.5%, at 21,135.77, while the AIM All-Share was up 0.67 of a point, 0.1%, at 714.09.

The Cboe UK 100 was up 0.6% at 991.28, the Cboe UK 250 was down 0.5% at 18,414.54, and the Cboe UK Small Companies Index was up 0.1% at 16,801.90.

In European equities on Tuesday, the CAC 40 in Paris closed up 0.2%, while the DAX 40 in Frankfurt ended down 0.1%.

Stocks in New York were mixed. The Dow Jones Industrial Average was up 0.3%, the S&P 500 index was slightly higher, but the Nasdaq Composite fell 0.4%.

Joshua Mahony at Scope Markets said: "The uncertainty surrounding the possibility of a breakthrough in negotiations between the US and Iran means investors are essentially guessing the outcome of discussions that we do not even know are taking place."

While US President Donald Trump pointed to "very good" talks to end the war with Iran, officials in Tehran denied that such discussions are taking place.

Tehran's parliamentary speaker said "no negotiations" were underway, insisting that Trump was seeking "to manipulate the financial and oil markets".

Mahony at Scope Markets said the chances of a swift deal "remain minimal according to many sources, with both sides bringing a raft of demands that the other side will likely reject. Therefore, it is the job of Trump to keep a lid on energy prices while the conflict and negotiations take place," he added.

"Essentially all roads lead to oil right now, and as long as Trump can keep crude prices stable we will see the likes of gold and equity declines minimised," Mahony continued.

Brent oil was quoted at USD103.95 a barrel at the time of the London equities close on Tuesday, up from USD102.07 late on Monday.

Oil majors provided support in London, with BP and Shell up 3.5% and 3.2% respectively.

Gold traded at USD4,421.77 an ounce on Tuesday, up against USD4,376.19 on Monday.

The yield on the US 10-year Treasury was quoted flat at 4.38%. The yield on the US 30-year Treasury was also unchanged at 4.94%.

The impact of the Iran war was laid bare in the UK purchasing managers' index.

S&P Global's flash UK services PMI business activity index fell to a 6-month low of 51.2 points in March from 53.9 in February, underperforming against FXStreet-cited market expectations of a milder fall to 53.0 points.

UK businesses reported a hit to customer demand from the war in the Middle East, as business activity expectations for the year ahead "eased considerably", S&P Global said, with optimism falling to the lowest since last June.

The flash manufacturing index declined to 51.4 points in March from 51.7 in February, beating expectations of a sharper fall to 51.1 points.

Manufacturers signalled the sharpest rise in input costs since October 2022, while the on-month acceleration of input price inflation across the UK manufacturing sector was the biggest since October 1992, following Black Wednesday the prior month.

Chris Williamson, chief business economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said: "The war in the Middle East has hit the UK economy in March, stalling growth while driving inflation sharply higher."

The survey is likely to "reinforce the sense that a large inflation shock is coming," JPMorgan analyst Allan Monks said.

In Europe, business activity in the eurozone also slowed significantly in March.

The HCOB Flash Eurozone purchasing managers' index registered a lower figure of 50.5 for March, down from 51.9 in February. A reading above 50 indicates growth.

Barclays said the report showed clear imprints of the latest developments in energy prices and rising tensions in the Middle East, with demand prospects appearing weaker, input prices increasing sharply, and signs of supply bottlenecks.

The pound was quoted slightly higher at USD1.3394 at the time of the London equities close on Tuesday, compared to USD1.3390 on Monday.

The euro stood at USD1.1591, higher against USD1.1579. Against the yen, the dollar was trading higher at JPY158.82 compared to JPY158.79.

UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves said the economic challenges from the Iran war may be "significant" and contingency planning is underway for energy bill support "for those who need it most".

Reeves said work is underway on targeted help for households when the current energy price cap expires at the end of June, at which point global prices could push up domestic bills.

"The challenges may be significant but I promise to do what is right and fair, being responsive in a changing world and responsible in the national interest," she added.

Meanwhile, Huw Pill, chief economist at the Bank of England, used a speech to say policymakers should not use the "fog of uncertainty" as a reason not to act to combat the risks of rising inflation due to the Iran war.

Pill said the UK's central bank stands "ready to act" if needed to rein in inflation as he warned he sees "upside risks to price stability mounting as a result of events in the Gulf".

On the FTSE 100, 3i Group rose 2.2% ahead of Action's capital markets day on Thursday. The Dutch discount retailer is 3i's largest investment.

Analysts at Citi note expect slowing like-for-like sales growth at Action to prove temporary, with rising inflation and consumer pressure instead reinforcing shifts towards discounted channels.

On the FTSE 250, Bellway slumped 18% after mixed first-half results and as investors worry about the impact of the Iran war.

The Newcastle-upon-Tyne-based housebuilder said the Middle East conflict "heightens the risk of both inflationary cost pressures and an impact to customer demand, and we have already seen volatility return to the mortgage market."

The crisis has seen hopes for interest rate cuts - seen as boosting the UK housing market - evaporate, with financial markets instead pricing in multiple hikes, as inflation expectations ramp up amid the jump in oil prices.

"It's fair to say 2026 is not playing out as housebuilders would have hoped or expected. A picture of gently easing interest rates, manageable cost inflation and recovering demand has been torn up by events in the Middle East," observed Dan Coatsworth, head of markets at AJ Bell.

"The longer the current situation goes on, the larger the likely impact on build cost inflation, buyer confidence in the property market, and on mortgage affordability and availability too," he added.

Meanwhile, Trustpilot fell 17% as Deutsche Bank confirmed that Advent Global Opportunities Master Ltd Partnership sold 21.6 million Trustpilot shares, a 5.6% stake, at 214 pence per share, worth GBP46 million. Advent Global is an entity closely associated with Mohammed Anjarwala, a non-executive director at Trustpilot.

The biggest risers on the FTSE 100 were BP, up 18.90p at 557.50p, BT Group, up 6.90p at 206.60p, Endeavour Mining, up 134.00p at 4,128.00p, Shell, up 105.50p at 3,460.00p and Convatec, up 5.80p at 223.40p.

The biggest fallers on the FTSE 100 were Persimmon, down 38.00p at 1,104.50p, JD Sports, down 2.04p at 67.30p, Experian, down 70.00p at 2,566.00p, Kingfisher, down 6.80p at 289.60p and Babcock International, down 28.00p at 1,242.00p.

Wednesday's global economic calendar has an Australian CPI print overnight, followed by UK inflation data.

Wednesday's UK corporate calendar has full-year results from TT Electronics, Wag Payment Solutions and Headlam Group.

By Jeremy Cutler, Alliance News reporter

Comments and questions to newsroom@alliancenews.com

Copyright 2026 Alliance News Ltd. All Rights Reserved.

US 2-Year Auction High Yield Rises From Previous Month, Demand Lower
French finance minister says tax cuts would not ease Iran war energy shock
data.description:
PARIS, March 24 (Reuters) - Tax cuts or fuel price subsidies are not an effective response to the energy price shock triggered by the Iran war and risk fuelling inflation, France's Finance Minister Roland Lescure told lawmakers on Tuesday.

The French government has faced pressure from opposition parties to cut value-added tax on fuel or provide other support, but has little room to manoeuvre because its budget deficit is among the biggest in the euro zone.

The Italian government cut excise duties on fuel last week and Spain approved a package worth 5 billion euros to try to soften the economic impact of the Middle East conflict.

"When (energy) supply is constrained, supporting demand through subsidies or tax cuts does not act on the availability of supplies and ultimately only amplifies inflation," Lescure told the lower house's finance commission.

Any support measures eventually taken, he said, must be targeted and limited in time.

So far, the government's response to what Lescure called "a new oil shock" has focused on coordinating an international release of strategic oil reserves and policing pump prices for gouging. It has also offered loans, relief from payroll contributions and flexible tax deadlines for the transport, fishing and farming sectors.

France, Lescure said, was less exposed to soaring oil and gas prices and better prepared compared with parts of Asia and some European neighbours. 

A permanent $10 rise in oil prices would shave around 0.1 percentage points off economic growth, Lescure said. If prices remain around $100 a barrel - about $35 above pre-crisis assumptions - growth could be reduced by 0.3 to 0.4 points, while inflation would rise by around one point.

He also called for vigilance over rising interest rates and said borrowing costs were higher, although France was finding financing without difficulty and had completed a third of its 2026 bond issuance programme. 

(Reporting by Leigh Thomas; editing by Barbara Lewis)

European markets cautious as they await clear signals on Iran
data.description:
(Alliance News) - On Tuesday, major European bourses closed the session on a cautious note, as markets remain skeptical regarding the prospects for de-escalation suggested on the eve by US President Donald Trump.

On the contrary, Israel continues its strikes and the Strait of Hormuz remains firmly under Tehran's jurisdiction.

On the macroeconomic front, economic growth in the Eurozone slowed in March, with flash composite PMI data signaling only marginal expansion in activity, the weakest in ten months.

According to the S&P Global Flash Eurozone Composite PMI, the economy recorded a value of 50.5 in March, compared to 51.9 in February, remaining above the 50 threshold that separates growth from contraction for the fifteenth consecutive month.

Consequently, the MIB closed the session up 0.4% at 43,369.53 points, the Mid-Cap shed 0.1% to 52,146.45, the Small-Cap edged into fractional red at 32,157.53, and Italy Growth gained 0.3% to 8,134.65 points.

Other European indices were mixed: the CAC 40 in Paris closed up 0.2%, the DAX 40 in Frankfurt recorded a 0.1% decrease, and the FTSE 100 in London rose 0.6%.

On the MIB, Inwit is considering legal action on multiple fronts, both civil and potentially criminal, in its ongoing dispute with Telecom Italia and Fastweb+Vodafone regarding the tower joint venture and the interpretation of Master Service Agreements (MSAs), which account for over 85% of the tower company's annual revenue. Inwit shares closed at the top of the basket, up 9.9% at EUR6.89 per share, while Telecom Italia fell 0.4% to EUR0.601 per share.

Meanwhile, Poste Italiane shed 0.3%.

In banking consolidation news, Crédit Agricole presented a minority list of seven candidates for the renewal of the board of Banco BPM, which was down fractionally at EUR11.655 per share. This signals a desire to play an active role in the Milanese bank's governance, while reiterating that it does not seek control and does not oppose the current board, even after exceeding the 20% threshold.

Leonardo, with daily sales of 2.1%, is moving toward exiting the Chinese joint venture Cnbm Aerostructure, launched in 2023 in Shanghai to support Comac's CR929 program, intended to compete with Boeing's Dreamliner.

The defense sector closed the basket with negative performances from Avio (down 4.9%), Fincantieri (down 3.7%), and Leonardo. Luxury stocks moved into the red, with Brunello Cucinelli and Ferrari both down 1.3%.

On the Mid-Cap, Pirelli gained 1.0%. However, a concern weighs on the board, as latest-generation high-end tires feature chips capable of geolocating the vehicle. Washington does not want this metadata to fall into the hands of Chinese government agencies, meaning the Bicocca-based company faces potential sanctions and blocks in a key market for its tires.

RCS MediaGroup - up 2.0% - approved its 2025 accounts, closing the year with a net profit of EUR54.8 million, down from EUR62 million the previous year. The board will propose a dividend of EUR0.07 per share to the shareholders' meeting, unchanged from the previous year.

MAIRE, which via Nextchem was awarded two feasibility studies in Southern Africa and Southeast Asia, closed trading down 1.5%.

Kkcg maritime launched a partial tender offer for Ferretti - down 0.7% on the day - aiming to increase its stake to 29.9% from 14.5%, in a context marked by the confrontation with Weichai, the reference shareholder via Fih, which rejected the offer.

On the Small-Cap, Banca Profilo approved its 2025 results, confirming the previously released preliminary data, and announced that the board will propose a dividend of EUR0.007, a decrease from the EUR0.0128 dividend paid in 2025. The stock rose 1.0% to EUR0.1545.

Cairo Communication - up 0.2% at the end of the session - closed 2025 with a group net profit of EUR39.7 million, down from EUR45.2 million as of December 31, 2024. The company proposed a dividend of EUR0.18 per share, up from EUR0.16 paid in 2024.

Sabaf closed 2025 with an adjusted group net profit of EUR12.9 million, down 19% from EUR16.0 million as of December 31, 2024. The company proposed a dividend of EUR0.58 per share, the same as in 2024. The stock gained 3.2%.

Zest - with daily sales of 0.80% - closed 2025 with a net loss of EUR966,000, compared to a profit of EUR7.6 million in 2024, which included a foreign exchange gain of EUR11.2 million. Revenue and income stood at EUR9.70 million, essentially stable compared to EUR9.71 million in 2024.

Among SMEs, EBidCo - a Xenon vehicle - exceeded the 95% threshold in Eles, reaching 98% at the opening of the sell-out window valid until April 14. After that date, the terms for the squeeze-out of any remaining shares will be triggered. For Eles, which fell 0.3%, delisting is imminent.

Alantra confirmed its "buy" recommendation and target price of EUR2.30 per share for expert.ai, citing "solid preliminary results for the 2025 financial year, exceeding our forecasts, and the update of the strategic plan." The stock rose 1.3% to EUR1.76 per share.

Circle, through its subsidiary eXyond, was awarded a EUR200,000 contract in logistics, and the stock responded positively, rising 3.6%.

Helyx Industries - up 5.6% at the close - announced it closed the 2025 financial year with a net loss of EUR4.4 million, an improvement over the EUR5.9 million loss in 2024.

In New York, the Dow Jones gained 0.3%, the Nasdaq shed 0.3%, and the S&P 500 moved up 0.1%.

On the currency front, the euro is trading at USD1.1587 from USD1.1584 on Monday evening, while the pound is trading at USD1.3388 from USD1.3394 yesterday evening.

Among commodities, Brent crude is trading at USD103.79 per barrel from USD102.62 on Monday evening, while gold is worth USD4,418.32 per ounce from USD4,374.19 yesterday evening.

On Wednesday's economic calendar, UK consumer price index data are expected at 0800 CET, while German business confidence will be measured at 1000 CET.

At 1130 CET, an auction of Italian two-year CTZs will be held, while from 1200 CET the focus shifts to US mortgages and, at 1330 CET, to the US trade balance.

In Piazza Affari, results are expected from 21 companies, including Fincantieri, Danieli & C, and Rino Petino.

By Michele Cirulli, Alliance News reporter

Comments and questions to redazione@alliancenews.com

Copyright 2026 Alliance News IS Italian Service Ltd. All rights reserved.

data.title: CERAWeek-Machado says Venezuela could eventually produce as much as 5 million bpd of crude oil
Global Business Activity Slows as Iran War Weighs -- 4th Update
data.description:
By Ed Frankl


Business activity in the U.S., Europe and parts of Asia slowed this month as energy prices and uncertainty were driven higher by the war in the Middle East, while a cooling of new orders pointed to longer-lasting harm if the conflict continues or escalates.

Data firm S&P Global said Tuesday that its U.S. composite Purchasing Managers' Index fell to an 11-month low of 51.4 in March, compared with 51.9 in February.

"The flash PMI survey data for March signal an unwelcome combination of slower growth and rising inflation following the outbreak of war in the Middle East," said Chris Williamson, chief business economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Prices for inputs spiked due to soaring energy prices, but costs for customers also jumped as selling prices reached a more than three-and-a-half-year high, S&P said. Employment fell for the first time in over a year, while supply delays were more widely reported than any time since October 2022, the survey responses said.

Following the pinch from tariffs, cuts to immigration and a weakening labor market, the spike in energy prices adds yet another headwind for growth and employment, said Oliver Allen, senior U.S. economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics.

"We remain of the view that the hit to activity and the labor market from the energy shock will be more powerful than the fleeting lift to core prices, and that Fed policy is more likely to be easier than tighter by the end of this year," he said.

Similar surveys released Tuesday pointed to a sharp slowdown in Europe.

A surge in energy and food prices could hit the eurozone economy hard. The European Central Bank last week projected inflation to average at 4.4% this year if oil prices peak at $145 a barrel. Eurozone inflation was 1.9% in February.

"The economic outlook has weakened significantly, but all now rests on how the conflict unfolds," ING economist Bert Colijn said in a note to clients.

Input-cost inflation in the eurozone rose at its fastest rate in just over three years, while the overall level of output growth was dragged by a deterioration in new orders, S&P said.

Meanwhile, activity expanded at its slowest rate since October 2022 in India, which relies heavily on energy imports that transit through the Strait of Hormuz, the waterway that has largely been closed as a result of the conflict. Before the war began, 60% of India's liquefied petroleum gas--used for cooking in households and restaurants--came from the Gulf, according to Capital Economics.

Input costs climbed to a near four-year high, with a range of items reported as up in price including aluminum, chemicals, electronic components, energy, food, iron ore, leather, oil, rubber and steel, S&P said.

Australia recorded the strongest fall in output since 2023, while Japanese activity also slowed, PMI data showed.

In the U.K., activity fell to a six-month low, as squeezed margins led to a further increase in job shedding, the survey showed. Unemployment has already inched higher in recent months, according to official data.

President Trump over the weekend threatened to hit Iran's power facilities unless the country fully reopened the Strait of Hormuz, through which around 20% of the world's oil supply typically flows. On Monday, Trump said he was postponing strikes against Iranian power plants for five days after what he called constructive talks with Iran, though Iranian leaders later said no such talks had taken place.

Investors had ramped up bets that central banks like the ECB would raise rates this year, amid skyrocketing energy prices prompted by the near-closure of the trade artery.

ECB Vice-President Luis de Guindos reiterated on Monday that the longer and more widespread the conflict, the greater its impact would be on the eurozone's economy. The central bank raised its inflation forecasts and lowered its growth expectations for this year.

In Europe, climbing oil and gas prices will likely threaten some of the recent revival in industry coming from Germany's fiscal stimulus. The country has unlocked around $1 trillion that meant industrial production picked up toward the end of 2025.

Growth in the eurozone's manufacturing industry accelerated in March--with weakness in the 21-nation currency area coming instead from the services sector--according to S&P's PMI data. However, some of that manufacturing boost could have come as firms sought to get ahead of predicted future supply-chain shocks.

Phil Smith, economics associate director at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said that demand in Germany had been boosted by companies reacting to the disruption brought on by the war, with some bringing forward purchases over concerns about potential supply disruption in the coming months.

Firms in the U.S. also appeared to be heeding warnings over the outlook.

"Companies are meanwhile building safety stocks amid concerns that the war may lead to more protracted supply issues and price rises while trimming headcounts to reduce overheads," said S&P's Williamson.

The World Trade Organization last week said the Middle East conflict could weigh heavily on trade and output. It penciled in growth in trade volumes this year at 1.9%, though at only 1.4% should the recent oil price increase persist throughout 2026. Either result would mark a sharp slowdown from 2025's 4.6% growth, which was propelled by the artificial-intelligence boom and frontloading of tariffs.


Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-24-26 1257ET
